Core Description: Magh Fair is organized at Prayagraj near the confluence of the Ganga, Yamuna, and Saraswati rivers during the month of Maagh. Devotees gather to take holy dips and perform religious rituals.
Cultural Significance: An important spiritual fair reflecting pilgrimage traditions and river worship in Hindu culture

Core Description: Rath Fair is organized in Vrindavan in honor of Lord Rangnath, drawing devotees for worship and ceremonial observances.
Cultural Significance: Associated with Vaishnav devotion and Braj religious traditions

Core Description: Gau Charan Fair, also known as Gopashtami, is celebrated on the eighth day of the Kartik month, during which cows are worshipped.
Cultural Significance: Reflects agrarian values and reverence for cattle in Hindu tradition

Core Description: Bhai Dooj fair celebrates the bond between brothers and sisters, rooted in the legend of Goddess Yamuna and Yamraj, with sisters praying for their brothers’ well-being.
Cultural Significance: Symbolizes familial love, respect, and social harmony

Core Description: Nauchandi Fair is held in Meerut where devotees worship Nauchandi Devi and also pay homage to Sufi saint Syed Salar.
Cultural Significance: A symbol of communal harmony blending Hindu and Muslim traditions

Core Description: Dewa Sharif Fair is organized at the shrine of Sufi saint Waris Ali Shah, attracting devotees from different faiths.
Cultural Significance: Promotes Sufi traditions and interfaith harmony

Core Description: Shakumbhari Fair is held during Navratri in honor of Goddess Shakumbhari, with religious rituals and gatherings.
Cultural Significance: Important Shakti worship festival in western Uttar Pradesh

column8: Kumbh Fair is a massive religious gathering held on a rotational basis at sacred river sites, where pilgrims perform ritual bathing and ascetic practices.
column9: Recognized by UNESCO as Intangible Cultural Heritage; represents faith, austerity, and collective spirituality
